subject: Physics
q: Three masses $200 \mathrm{~kg}, 300 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 400 kg are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides 20 m. They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side 25 m and with the same centre. The work done in this process $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Gravitational constant $\mathrm{G}=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$)
question: Three masses $200 \mathrm{~kg}, 300 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 400 kg are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides 20 m. They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side 25 m and with the same centre. The work done in this process $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Gravitational constant $\mathrm{G}=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$)
solution:
Work done equals negative change in gravitational potential energy.
For three masses in an equilateral triangle, gravitational PE is $U = -G\left(\frac{m_1m_2}{r_{12}} + \frac{m_2m_3}{r_{23}} + \frac{m_1m_3}{r_{13}}\right)$.
Initial configuration (side 20 m):
$U_i = -\frac{G}{20}(200 \times 300 + 300 \times 400 + 200 \times 400)$
$= -\frac{260000G}{20} = -13000G$
Final configuration (side 25 m):
$U_f = -\frac{G}{25}(260000) = -10400G$
Work done:
$W = -(U_f - U_i) = -10400G + 13000G$
$= 2600G = 2600 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-11}$
$= 1.742 \times 10^{-7}$ J $\approx 1.74 \times 10^{-7}$ J

subject: Physics
q: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, a body weighed $300 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface of earth. How much it would weigh at R/4 depth under surface of earth ?
question: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, a body weighed $300 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface of earth. How much it would weigh at R/4 depth under surface of earth ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { At surface: } \mathrm{mg}=300 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=\frac{300}{\mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}} \\ & \text { At Depth } \frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}: \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{s}}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{R}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{s}}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4 \mathrm{R}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}}{4}\end{aligned}$
weight at depth $\quad=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{m} \times \frac{3 \mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}}{4} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} \times 300 \\ & =225 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A spaceship orbits around a planet at a height of 20 km from its surface. Assuming that only gravitational field of the planet acts on the spaceship, what will be the number of complete revolutions made by the spaceship in 24 hours around the planet?
[Given: Mass of planet  =8×1022 kg ,
Radius of planet =2×106 m,
Gravitational constant  G=6.67×10-11 Nm2/kg2 ]
question:
A spaceship orbits around a planet at a height of 20 km from its surface. Assuming that only gravitational field of the planet acts on the spaceship, what will be the number of complete revolutions made by the spaceship in 24 hours around the planet?
[Given: Mass of planet  =8×1022 kg ,
Radius of planet =2×106 m,
Gravitational constant  G=6.67×10-11 Nm2/kg2 ]
solution:
T=2π(R+h)3GM=2π 8.242×10186.67×10-11×8×1022 
T≈7809 sec
Number of revolutions in 24 hours:
n=864007809=11.06
≈ 11 revolutions

subject: Physics
q: A satellite of mass $\frac{M}{2}$ is revolving around earth in a circular orbit at a height of $\frac{R}{3}$ from earth surface. The angular momentum of the satellite is $M \sqrt{\frac{G M R}{x}}$. The value of $x$ is ______ , where $M$ and $R$ are the mass and radius of earth, respectively. ( G is the gravitational constant)
question: A satellite of mass $\frac{M}{2}$ is revolving around earth in a circular orbit at a height of $\frac{R}{3}$ from earth surface. The angular momentum of the satellite is $M \sqrt{\frac{G M R}{x}}$. The value of $x$ is ______ , where $M$ and $R$ are the mass and radius of earth, respectively. ( G is the gravitational constant)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { orbital velocity } v_0=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{4 \mathrm{R} / 3}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{GM}}{4 \mathrm{R}}} \\ & \text { Angular momentum of satellite }=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{v}_0 \frac{4 R}{3} \\ & =\frac{M}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{GM}}{4 \mathrm{R}}} \cdot \frac{4 \mathrm{R}}{3} \\ & =M \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GMR}}{3}} \\ & x=3\end{aligned}$ (ii) Since mass of satellite is comparable to the mass of earth.
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\text { G.M. } \frac{\mathrm{M}}{2}}{\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{R}}{3}\right)^2}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \omega^2 \cdot \frac{8 \mathrm{R}}{9} \\ & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{81 \mathrm{GM}}{128 \mathrm{R}^3}}\end{aligned}$ Angular momentum of satellite about common centre of mass, $\begin{aligned} & L=\frac{M}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{8 R}{9}\right)^2 \cdot \omega \\ & L=M \sqrt{\operatorname{GMR}\left(\frac{8}{81}\right)} \\ & x=\frac{81}{8} \simeq 10 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> around earth, while a second satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage difference in the time periods of the two satellites is:
question: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> around earth, while a second satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage difference in the time periods of the two satellites is:
solution:
T2∝R3

T=kR3/2

dTT=32dRR

=32×0.02=0.03

%Change =3%

subject: Physics
q: Two planets $A$ and $B$ having masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ move around the sun in circular orbits of $r_1$ and $r_2$ radii respectively. If angular momentum of $A$ is $L$ and that of $B$ is $3 \mathrm{~L}$, the ratio of time period $\left(\frac{T_A}{T_B}\right)$ is:
question: Two planets $A$ and $B$ having masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ move around the sun in circular orbits of $r_1$ and $r_2$ radii respectively. If angular momentum of $A$ is $L$ and that of $B$ is $3 \mathrm{~L}$, the ratio of time period $\left(\frac{T_A}{T_B}\right)$ is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2}{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_1} \ldots \ldots .(1) \\ & \frac{\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2}{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~L}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_2} \ldots \ldots .(2) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}}=3 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^2 \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^3 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{27} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1}\right)^3=\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\end{aligned}$

q: The elongation of a wire on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The same wire of same dimensions is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on another planet. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The elongation of a wire on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The same wire of same dimensions is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on another planet. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Change in length ∝Force∝mg

mg=kx=YAlx

Thus, x∝g

Then, 10-46×10-5=gg' ⇒ g'=0.6g=6 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: The value of acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is <math><mi> </mi><mn>9.8</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The altitude above its surface at which the acceleration due to gravity decreases to <math><mi> </mi><mn>4.9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is close to: (Radius of earth <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> )
question: The value of acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is <math><mi> </mi><mn>9.8</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The altitude above its surface at which the acceleration due to gravity decreases to <math><mi> </mi><mn>4.9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is close to: (Radius of earth <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> )
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity g=GMR2 .......(1)
⇒g2=GM(R+h)2 ............(2)
From (1) and (2),
GM2R2=GM(R+h)2
R+h=2R
h=0.414×6.4×106
=2.6×106 m

subject: Physics
q: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
question: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}^2 \propto \mathrm{R}^3 \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R} / 9}\right)^3 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}=(3)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{s}}=\left(\frac{27}{27}\right)=1 \text { day }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The weight of a body on the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then weight of the body when taken to a depth half of the radius of the earth will be:
question: The weight of a body on the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then weight of the body when taken to a depth half of the radius of the earth will be:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity changes with depth from the surface, which is given by

g'=g1-dR   ...(i)

The given data is 

W=400 Nd=R2

Multiplying equation (i) with the mass we get the weight.

mg'=mg1-R2R=mg12⇒W'=400 N2=200 N

question: The initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> required to project a body vertically upward from the surface of the earth to reach a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>R</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth, may be described in terms of escape velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></mfrac></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub></math>. ⇒v=20GM11R

subject: Physics
q: The height at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{9}$ (where $\mathrm{g}=$ the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth) in terms of $R$, the radius of the earth is
question: The height at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{9}$ (where $\mathrm{g}=$ the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth) in terms of $R$, the radius of the earth is
solution:
$g^{\prime}=\frac{G M}{(R+h)^2}$, acceleration due to gravity at height $h$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{g}{9}=\frac{G M}{R^2} \cdot \frac{R^2}{(R+h)^2}=g\left(\frac{R}{R+h}\right)^2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{9}=\left(\frac{R}{R+h}\right)^2 \Rightarrow \frac{R}{R+h}=\frac{1}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow 3 R=R+h \Rightarrow 2 R=h
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: If a body of mass $1$ kg falls on the earth from infinity, it attains velocity $(v)$ and kinetic energy $(k)$ on reaching the surface of earth. The values of $v$ and $k$ respectively are _______. (Take radius of earth to be $6400$ km and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$)
question: If a body of mass $1$ kg falls on the earth from infinity, it attains velocity $(v)$ and kinetic energy $(k)$ on reaching the surface of earth. The values of $v$ and $k$ respectively are _______. (Take radius of earth to be $6400$ km and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
By conservation of mechanical energy, the total energy of the body at infinity is zero.

When the body reaches the surface of the earth, its potential energy is $-\dfrac{GMm}{R}$ and kinetic energy is $k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.

$0 = k - \dfrac{GMm}{R}$

$k = \dfrac{GMm}{R} = mgR$

Substituting $m = 1$ kg, $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$, and $R = 6400 \times 10^3$ m:

$k = 1 \times 9.8 \times 6400 \times 10^3 = 6.272 \times 10^7$ J

The velocity $v$ is given by $\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = k$.

$v = \sqrt{2gR} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 6400 \times 10^3}$

$v = \sqrt{125.44 \times 10^6} = 11.2 \times 10^3$ m/s $= 11.2$ km/s

Answer: $11.2$ km/s; $6.27 \times 10^7$ J

subject: Physics
q: A very long (length L) cylindrical galaxy is made of uniformly distributed mass and has radius R (R<<L). A star outside the galaxy is orbiting the galaxy in a plane perpendicular to the galaxy and passing through its centre. If the time period of the star is T and its distance from the galaxy's axis is r, then
question: A very long (length L) cylindrical galaxy is made of uniformly distributed mass and has radius R (R<<L). A star outside the galaxy is orbiting the galaxy in a plane perpendicular to the galaxy and passing through its centre. If the time period of the star is T and its distance from the galaxy's axis is r, then
solution:
Due to a long solid cylinder gravitational field strength can be given as,

g= 2 G λr

Where,

λ= linear mass density of galaxy.  

For the orbital motion around the galaxy,



fg=fcentripetal

⇒       mg=m ω2r 

⇒       2Gλr=ω2r

⇒      ω2 ∝1r2

⇒      ω ∝1r

⇒      2πT ∝1r     ⇒     T∝r

So option B is correct.

subject: Physics
q: The mass density of a spherical body is given by <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> for <math><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> for <math><mi>r</mi><mo>></mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the distance from the center. The correct graph that describes qualitatively the acceleration, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> of a test particle as a function of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is:
question: The mass density of a spherical body is given by <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> for <math><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> for <math><mi>r</mi><mo>></mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the distance from the center. The correct graph that describes qualitatively the acceleration, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> of a test particle as a function of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is:
solution:
For Inside point r≤R

g=E=Gr2∫0r4πr2drKr=GKr2×4πr22=2πGK=constant

For outside point r>R

E=gE=GMr2E=Gr2∫0R(4πr2dr')(Kr')E=4πKGr2[r'22]0R

E=2πKGr2R2

E∝1r2.

subject: Physics
q:
Statement I : The law of gravitation holds good for any pair of bodies in the universe.

Statement II : The weight of any person becomes zero when the person is at the centre of the earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Statement I : The law of gravitation holds good for any pair of bodies in the universe.

Statement II : The weight of any person becomes zero when the person is at the centre of the earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
Gravitational force of attraction exists in all the places in the universe. This is the force of attraction between any two substances that are having a mass of their own. 

Every object in the universe attracts every other object with a force which is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

The weight of a body placed at the centre of the earth is zero. This is because acceleration due to gravity is zero at the centre and hence weight mg is zero at the centre. Thus, both statements are true.

subject: Physics
q: The weight of a body on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The gravitational force on it when taken at a height, from the surface of earth, equal to one-fourth the radius of the earth is:
question: The weight of a body on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The gravitational force on it when taken at a height, from the surface of earth, equal to one-fourth the radius of the earth is:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity changes as one goes to a height from the surface of the earth. Let the new acceleration due to gravity be g'. The formula for acceleration due to gravity at a height is given by g'=gR2R+h2

The weight of the body on surface is W=mg=100 N

The weight at the given height is W=mg'.

⇒W'=100×R2R+R42⇒W'=100×R225R216=64 N

subject: Physics
q: A test particle is moving in a circular orbit in the gravitational field produced by a mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Identify the current relation between the radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> of the particle’s orbit and its period <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>:</mo></math>
question: A test particle is moving in a circular orbit in the gravitational field produced by a mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Identify the current relation between the radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> of the particle’s orbit and its period <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>:</mo></math>
solution:
M=∫ρ4πr2dr
=∫0RKr24πr2dr
=4πKR
Satellite velocity =GMR=4πK
Time period, T=2πRv=2πR4πK
Now,
TR=2π4πK= constant

subject: Physics
q: Which graph correctly presents the variation of acceleration due to gravity with the distance from the centre of the earth (radius of the earth $=R_E$ )?
question: Which graph correctly presents the variation of acceleration due to gravity with the distance from the centre of the earth (radius of the earth $=R_E$ )?
solution: Acceleration due to gravity, $g \propto r$ (if $r < R_E$ ) and $g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}\left(\right.$ if $\left.r>R_E\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A planet having mass 9 Me and radius 4Re, where Me and Re are mass and radius of earth respectively, has escape velocity in km s-1 given by: (Given escape velocity on earth Ve=11.2×103 m s-1)
question: A planet having mass 9 Me and radius 4Re, where Me and Re are mass and radius of earth respectively, has escape velocity in km s-1 given by: (Given escape velocity on earth Ve=11.2×103 m s-1)
solution:
The escape velocity on earth is given by Ve =2GMeRe

Escape velocity for the planet is  

V'esc=2G(9Me)4Re   ...(i)

The escape velocity of earth is Ve=11.2×103 m s-1

From equation (i)

V'esc=942GMeRe=32×11.2×103 m s-1⇒V'esc=16.8 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: Suppose that the angular velocity of rotation of the Earth is increased. Then, as a consequence,
question: Suppose that the angular velocity of rotation of the Earth is increased. Then, as a consequence,
options: weight of the object, everywhere on the earth, will decrease.
weight of the object, everywhere on the earth, will increase.
except at poles, weight of the object on the earth, will decrease.
there will be no change in weight anywhere on the earth.
solution: Effective acceleration due to gravity, <math><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>′</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>R</mi><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow></mrow></math>, where <math><mi>ϕ</mi></math> is latitude. There will be no change in gravity at poles as <math><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math>. At other points as <math><mi>ω</mi></math> increases, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></math> will decrease.

subject: Physics
q: If the Earth has no rotational motion, the weight of a person on the equator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>. Determine the speed with which the earth would have to rotate about its axis so that the person at the equator will weigh <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>W</mi></math>. The radius of the Earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>
question: If the Earth has no rotational motion, the weight of a person on the equator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>. Determine the speed with which the earth would have to rotate about its axis so that the person at the equator will weigh <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>W</mi></math>. The radius of the Earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Due to the rotation of the earth,

Changed gravitational acceleration, g′=g-ω2Rcos2⁡θ

At the equator, angle of inclination, θ=0 

3g4=g-ω2R

w2R=g4

w= g4R

= 104×6400×103

=12×8×100

=11600=116×10-2=0.6×10-3 rad s-1.

question: On the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from the origin, the gravitational field due to a mass distribution is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. question: Suppose the gravitational force varies inversely as the nth power of distance. Then the time period planet in circular orbit of radius $\mathrm{R}$ around the sun will be proportional to
solution: $\mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{R}^{(\mathrm{n}+1) / 2}$

subject: Physics
q:
A satellite of mass 1000 kg is launched to revolve around the earth in an orbit at a height of 270 km from the earth's surface. Kinetic energy of the satellite in this orbit is ______ $\times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}$.
(Mass of earth $=6 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$, Radius of earth $=$ $6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$, Gravitational constant $=$
$\left.6.67 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{~kg}^{-2}\right)$
question:
A satellite of mass 1000 kg is launched to revolve around the earth in an orbit at a height of 270 km from the earth's surface. Kinetic energy of the satellite in this orbit is ______ $\times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}$.
(Mass of earth $=6 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$, Radius of earth $=$ $6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$, Gravitational constant $=$
$\left.6.67 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{~kg}^{-2}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{2 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{h}\right)} \\ & =\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24} \times 6.4 \times 10^6}{2\left(6.4 \times 10^6+2.7 \times 10^5\right)}=3 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The height ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>’ at which the weight of a body will be the same as that at the same depth ‘h’ from the surface of the earth is (Radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> and effect of the rotation of the earth is neglected)
question: The height ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>’ at which the weight of a body will be the same as that at the same depth ‘h’ from the surface of the earth is (Radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> and effect of the rotation of the earth is neglected)
solution:
GM(R+h)2=GMR3(R-h)
R3=(R+h)2(R-h)
=R2+h2+2hR(R-h)
R3=R3+h2R+2hR2-R2h-h3-2h2R
h3+h2(2R-R)-R2h=0
h3+h2R-R2h=0
h2+hR-R2=0
h=-R±R2+4(1)R22
=-R+5R2
=(5-1)2R

subject: Physics
q: To project a body of mass $m$ from earth's surface to infinity, the required kinetic energy is (assume, the radius of earth is $R_E, g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth):
question: To project a body of mass $m$ from earth's surface to infinity, the required kinetic energy is (assume, the radius of earth is $R_E, g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth):
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{e}}^2=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}} \\ & \mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}^2} \\ & \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{mgR}_{\mathrm{E}}\end{aligned}$

solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at a height h from the surface of the Earth,
g'=g11+hR2
Given: h=2R
∴g'=g11+22
or g'=g9

q: The escape velocity for a body projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth is $11 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. If the body is projected at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the vertical, the escape velocity will be
question: The escape velocity for a body projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth is $11 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. If the body is projected at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the vertical, the escape velocity will be
solution: Escape velocity of a body is independent of the angle of projection. Hence, changing the angle of projection is not going to effect the magnitude of escape velocity

subject: Physics
q: Net gravitational force at the center of a square is found to be $F_{1}$ when four particles having mass $M, 2 M, 3 M$ and $4 M$ are placed at the four corners of the square as shown in figure and it is $F_{2}$ when the positions of $3 M$ and $4 M$ are interchanged. The ratio $\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{5}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Net gravitational force at the center of a square is found to be $F_{1}$ when four particles having mass $M, 2 M, 3 M$ and $4 M$ are placed at the four corners of the square as shown in figure and it is $F_{2}$ when the positions of $3 M$ and $4 M$ are interchanged. The ratio $\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{5}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Let $k = \frac{G}{r^2\sqrt{2}}$ where $r = a/\sqrt{2}$.
Net field at center has components along $x$ and $y$.
Config 1 (original):
$g_x = k(-M+2M+3M-4M) = 0$,
$g_y = k(-M-2M+3M+4M) = 4kM$.
$F_1 = 4kM$.
Config 2 (3M and 4M swapped):
$g_x = k(-M+2M+4M-3M) = 2kM$,
$g_y = k(-M-2M+4M+3M) = 4kM$.
$F_2 = kM\sqrt{4+16} = 2\sqrt{5}\,kM$.
$\frac{F_1}{F_2} = \frac{4}{2\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow \alpha = 2$.

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
question: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
solution:
From angular momentum conservation

mV1r1=mV2r2

r1r2=v2v1=16

1:6

subject: Physics
q:
The percentage decrease in the weight of a rocket, when taken to a height of 32 km above the surface of earth will, be
(Radius of earth =6400 km)
question:
The percentage decrease in the weight of a rocket, when taken to a height of 32 km above the surface of earth will, be
(Radius of earth =6400 km)
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at a height h<<R is

g'=g1-2hR

Now, change in g is 

∴Δgg=2hR

Percentage change is 

⇒Δgg×100=2hR×100

=2×326400×100=1%

The speed of each particle is
question: Four particles, each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and equidistant from each other, move along a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle is
solution:
F=F1=F2=GM22R2

F 3 = GM 2 4 R 2

∴  FTotal towards the centre.

=2 F+F3

=2GM22R2+GM24R2

= GM 2 4 R 2 2 2 + 1

F T = F CP

GM24R222+1=Mv2R

∴    v = GM 4 R 2 2 + 1 .

q: A box weighs <math><mn>196</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> on a spring balance at the north pole. Its weight recorded on the same balance if it is shifted to the equator is close to (Take <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the north pole and the radius of the earth <math><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ):
question: A box weighs <math><mn>196</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> on a spring balance at the north pole. Its weight recorded on the same balance if it is shifted to the equator is close to (Take <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the north pole and the radius of the earth <math><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ):
solution:
At pole, weight mg=196
m=19.6kg
At equator, weight =mg-mω2R
=196-19.62π24×36002×6400×103
=195.32N
